chapter_title: Type Conversion, Type Aliases, and Type Deduction

cards.back: A type conversion requested explicitly by the programmer using a cast operator. This tells the compiler to perform a conversion that might not happen automatically or that you want to make explicit.

cards.text: Explicit type conversion is requested using a {{c1::cast operator}}. This process is also called {{c2::casting}} or {{c3::type casting}}.

cards.front: What is the syntax for static_cast?

cards.text: To cast a double to int using static_cast: {{c1::static_cast<int>}}(myDouble)

cards.question: What does this print?
cards.explanation: x is cast to double (10.0). The usual arithmetic conversions then convert y to double, so we get 10.0 / 4.0 = 2.5 instead of integer division.

cards.front: Why is static_cast preferred over C-style casts?
cards.back:
static_cast is safer and more visible:
  • Easier to search for in code
  • More restrictive (won't do dangerous conversions)
  • Compiler checks that the cast is valid
  • Makes intent explicit and clear
  • Catches some errors at compile-time

cards.text: A C-style cast looks like {{c1::(int)3.5}} or {{c2::int(3.5)}}. These should be {{c3::avoided}} in favor of static_cast.

cards.question: What does this code print?
cards.explanation: First line prints 'a' as a character. Second line casts char to int, printing its ASCII value (97).

static_cast is commonly used to:
  • Convert int to float/double for floating-point division
  • Convert char to int to print ASCII values
  • Make implicit conversions explicit for clarity
  • Convert between related pointer types
  • Perform narrowing conversions intentionally
examples: double d = static_cast<double>(x) / y;  // float division
int ascii = static_cast<int>('A');       // print ASCII value

cards.front: How can you use static_cast to avoid warnings when comparing signed and unsigned values?
cards.back:
Cast the signed value to unsigned (or vice versa) to make both operands the same type:
if (static_cast<unsigned int>(s) == u) { }

cards.front: Does static_cast perform runtime safety checks on conversions?
cards.back: No. static_cast performs the conversion at compile-time with no runtime checks. It's up to the programmer to ensure the conversion is safe and makes sense.

cards.question: What's the purpose of static_cast here?
cards.answer: To explicitly perform a narrowing conversion that would otherwise be rejected by brace initialization.
cards.explanation: Brace initialization disallows narrowing conversions. Using static_cast signals that the programmer intentionally wants to truncate the double to int.

cards.text: {{c1::static_cast}} is preferred over {{c2::C-style casts}} because it's safer, more visible, and easier to search for in code.
